title: A novel technique for implementing the finite element method in a shallow water equation
author: Swastika, Fakhruddin, et al.
materials: The discrete forms given by [[8], [20]] can be written in the(15)(M00Mnc)גˆ‚tY(t)=(0Sגˆ’gMֲ¯0)Y(t)where the element of mass matrices M,Mnc and stiffness S formulated asY(t)=[־·1,־·2,ג€¦,־·NV,ֿ•1,ֿ•2,ג€¦,ֿ•NS]T,Mnc=[mkj*],mkj*=גˆ«־©pֿˆk(x)ֿˆj(x)d־©p,M=[mkj],mkj=גˆ«־©pTk(x)Tj(x)d־©p,Mֲ¯=[mkj],mkj=גˆ«־©pTk(x)ֿˆj(x)d־©p,S=[skj],skj=גˆ«־©pd(x)גˆ‡ֿˆk(x)גˆ‡Tj(x)d־©p.where the integral can be evaluated numerically using the trapezoidal method. The term d(x) in S appears when we consider the varying topography. Furthermore, the R.H.S of [20] contains the multiplication between two different bases where both the base functions and test functions sum to 1.0 everywhere, and all terms in the mass matrix of an element will sum to the surface area of that element [31]. Then, we can use the mass lumping technique to obtain Mֲ¯=Mnc see the detailed description in [[26], [27], [31]]. Furthermore, the resulting scheme [12] is an ordinary differential system that can be solved by any time integration method. The procedure of P1NCגˆ’P1finite element pair approach for solving 2D- SWE is described as follows:1.Derive the variational problem of 2D SWE in primitive form to reformulated the weak form2.Employs the Galerkin procedure to get the discrete form. Here, we approximate the free surface ־· as a linear combination of conformal linear basis P1 and the velocity potential ֿ• as a linear combination of non-conformal linear basis P1NC.3.Compute the mass and stiffness matrix using any numerical integration method.4.Solve the discrete form which is ODE system using any time integration method. Here we use Crank-Nicholson time integration scheme.5.The P1NCגˆ’P1finite element pair is evaluated by comparing the numerical results to the exact results. This comparison is quantified with error and convergence rate.

steps.title: P1NCגˆ’P1 finite element method
steps.instruction:
In order to derive the scheme in the finite element sense, we start by formulating the variational problem of the governing equations [[1], [16]]. Let ־©p=[xmin,xmax]ֳ—[ymin,ymax] be the spatial domain with boundary גˆ‚־©p. Suppose the test functions of the equations [16] and [1] are V(x), W(x) in suitable test spaces E and P respectively. If we multiply [[1], [16]] by V(x), W(x) respectively, and integrate along the computational domain ־©p, we obtain the weak form(7)גˆ«־©pגˆ‚t־·Vd־©p=גˆ’גˆ«־©pגˆ‡.(d0גˆ‡ֿ•)Vd־©p(8)גˆ«־©pגˆ‚tֿ•Wd־©p=גˆ’gגˆ«־©p־·Wd־©pfor flat d(x)=d0. If we extend the R.H.S. of [30] using Gauss's theorem, we obtain the expression containing the velocity potential gradient, representing the contribution of the employed boundary conditions on גˆ‚־©p. To remove the velocity potential gradient, we employed a hard-wall boundary and obtained the weak form: determine

steps.title: P1NCגˆ’P1 finite element method
steps.instruction: In the derivation of the finite element scheme, we use a Galerkin procedure to approximate the weak form [[11], [26]] by writing ־· as a linear combination of conformal linear basis P1 or standard linear basis {T(x)}0NVand by writing ֿ• as a linear combination of non-conformal linear basis P1NCor simply, discontinuous linear basis {ֿˆ(x)}0NS such that(11)־·(x,t)ג‰…גˆ‘k=1NV־·k(t)Tk(x),(12)ֿ•(x,t)ג‰…גˆ‘k=1NSֿ•k(t)ֿˆk(x),where ־·k,ֿˆk denote nodal values and NS,NV denote the number of segments and vertices of the triangles respectively. The discontinuous linear basis {ֿˆ(x)}0NS and standard linear basis {T(x)}0NV is illustrated as in Fig.ֲ 1.Fig. 1The basis function used in P1NCגˆ’P1 finite element method. (left) P1NC basis function redrawn from Daniel Y. Le Roux. [30]., (right) P1 basis function.Fig 1

description: This section describes the P1NCגˆ’P1 finite element method, including the mathematical model and finite element discretization.
